author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: It took me 30 hours of YouTube instructional videos to learn enough FreeCAD to get something done. I'm sitting on 5 years of usage now. It has taken me 3 years of constant exposure over time to only begin to understand Blender. When I had the fundamentals of Blender down, I still needed two very in depth instruction secessions to find out what was going wrong. Give Mango Jelly Solutions a go for FreeCAD and Blender Base Camp for Blender. These guys are a wealth of knowledge. Thank you Dr. Smith.

author: @GavinImpett
text: I would say, this video landed as an "introduction on how to think like a human being," tutorial. I think in terms of separating process from production, which is not so easy with these devices. For instance, I had to do a search on cnc to know what type of device you are talking about while presenting the device. I've deep enough of a dumpster dive on 3d printing to think, "subtractive, not additive, chisel, not clay, right. Carry on." How to think with Tinkercad, makes transitioning to FreeCad a continuation of the thought process, it's just another tool.  Given this is a new piece of kit to you, how do you go about thinking if you are the problem, the machine, or some interaction. How do you kludge? One of the things my mum said to me that stuck was, "what I don't understand is where the computer ends and the internet begins." Looking forward to more.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like what your mum said mostly because I am not sure thinking is what happens in your head.

replies.author: @GavinImpett
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Ha! If we are talking about my head specifically, I agree, as for the rest of our species, I'm very much in the Dawkins/Dennett school. Or as Kent's second most famous resident put it, "It is interesting to contemplate a tangled bank," or in this case, the unbroken chain of being from a pre-human ancestor taking a rock to a mollusk, to your latest gadget. Which to my eyes, looks like someone forgot to put a door on a microwave.  It turns out one of my students did the English translation of one of the higher end 3d printers. He said to me, "Oh, you bought something that has the appearance of a 3d printer." Well yeah. So now I'm on 3d printer 4, somewhat the same as printer 1, only with a new extruder, motherboard, slicer, and a rebuilt pulley system,  thinking I really am a thoughtless idiot because I've built enough computers to recognize a defective motherboard, and instead of figuring it out myself, why did it not occur to do an internet search for a local 3d printer repair shop and save myself months of nonproductive frustration.  My direct experience tells me two out of three  3d printers ship defective in some way. My guess is as an engineer, you fix most of these defects without noticing they are there. Putting on your beginner's mind for a new piece of software and new kit is awesome and clearly you have sense enough not to pick up a spanner made of tpu, but I don't and I bet I'm not the only one. How do you know the car you are driving down the road with AKA printer is a working model?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @GavinImpett You know that's a good point and I was thinking of this recently because folks often complain of a problem with stuff that I just don't see. I never thought about it that way - but you could well be right I just 'put things right' without thinking because I have done it so often and it doesn't seem a problem to me - I will try and pay more attention to that inn future - thanks for the heads up on something I probably do without realizing - cheers mate

author: @ch5139
text: I’m glad you’ve found FreeCAD - more people need it!  A massive shout out should go to the team who created it. Giving their time and expertise over the years to create something that allows hobbyists and makers to have access to the tools only businesses could afford. I used AutoCAD along side real drawing boards starting at college in the 90’s, CIMCAD (for us oldies!), Solidworks - costing thousands per licence, and then the annual ‘maintenance’ payments😢. I for one am truly grateful to the open source software community and the generosity of it creators.  I still have a drawing board 🤫

author: @theurtleproject
text: totally agree with your approach to CAD …without a practical goal  in mind, you will just end up reading manuals with zero comprehension. I remember back in the dark ages of my youth, wadding through the excel manual trying to understand the whole system prior to starting…what an utter waste of time! Eventually I just started to try to achieve the job I had been assigned, and within a short period of time I had a working solution….not elegant but workable. A better understanding came later.

.svg files, right? 🤔 I believe I used Inkscape (open-source) to make my vector drawings and then export the paths as an .svg file. Maybe I'm mistaken, but the vector drawing aspect is very streamlined in Inkscape if you ever need that sort of ability anyhow. 

Thanks for sharing Rob! ✌️ Im stoked for this CNC journey you're on. I want one myself.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Scalable vector graphics are great for laser cutters. STL files are common in 3D as the are mesh maps of a 3D surface. The other favorite is STP which has more data on the design of the object but can't be 3D printed as they don't describe the surface - or at least that's what i believe

replies.author: @orpheuscreativeco9236
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkeringThanks for the response and for the leads. 👍  I'm going to look into mesh maps, sounds super useful. I can't wait to see what you dream up Rob!

author: @jdog0006
text:
Perhaps it has just sailed right over my head, but there is one point that I just don't get. Why are you moving to something else when you are already so good and familiar with Tinkercad? I don't use it. But that is just me. You are already adept at using Tinkercad and it supports 3D file types. 

I have used Blender for years and years. There came a time when I wanted to switch to a solid modelling platform. I tried to learn Freecad. But it was just so foreign to me because I was so used to using Blender. That is a personal limitation for me. It doesn't amount to a hill of beans in the real world. But while I was trying to learn Freecad, I noticed that it did support a whole host of filetypes. So if file type is an issue, perhaps you could stick with what you are already comfortable with and just use Freecad to convert to other filetypes?

I will add the direction that I chose to go for full disclosure. This is not meant to be an advertisement. Just my honest take. I researched many CAD softwares. There are a lot out there. As is often the case, price has a big influence on choice. I eventually chose Plasticity. It made the most sense for me. A license for Plasticity was a one time purchase at a reasonable price (at least as of today). This was a big part of the decision. But it was also very very similar to Blender. The moment that I purchased it, I was able to start using it immediately.

author: @chris993361
text: As someone who loves and uses freecad as one of multiple programs he uses, I will say it's fantastic but for a lot of people it's likely to have the steepest learning curve. It's worth it in the long run to support its open source growth and because it is very capable, but I don't want someone to start with it and then quit  3d modeling in general because they thought it was too hard.

Maybe I just work on really complicated geometries and most people won't have that issue.

author: @FuhrChris
text:
I've not had to do CAD/CAM in a while.
Back in my time.
The last stages of thinking in CAD/CAM is you code in a way that starts with your HOGGING bit, then you move onto other more refined or specialized bits.  
After you have worked through things like notching bits, you move onto things like finishing bits.
(Finishing bits are both the move delicate and EXPENSIVE.)  So learn to use them last and sparingly.
(After you have done this for a while you see the stages in which there is the loaded blank of metal.
Then there is the hogging out and in your mind you see where the machine will stop and wait for a tool Bit change out.
From there (Let's jump to a finishing bit for simplifications) The CAD/CAM doesn't normally just go all around the part it has to be told to raise up and move over to a place where it will sometimes plundge down other times is lowers itself beside the part and then moves in to put just a small finishing touch.)
( You should make a you tube about not just that sequence and moment but that you train your brain to THINK and prep your software file for these sequence's.) 

(Small personal note:  Outside of the UK we don't use the word "Bits" the way you do.
When instructing about CAD/CAM switch the words you use like "Components" or "parts" or something.  Your words are colliding into a mash up of "Bits" meaning Components and at the same time "Bits" means for many of us a short cut for "Drill Bits" and "Cutter Bit" which are also called "Cutter heads")

Your way 
You use Bits on Bits.

A more Refined way:
You load Bits into the CNC to make parts.

Do let me know the IF the new software makes it so easy that you don't need to learn that way of thinking anymore?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: will do mate - your advice is always appreciated - cheers

author: @FuhrChris
text:
You are adding a shadow across your chin.

You need to add two more lights at about the 30 degree angles one on the left and one on the right.  
(Possibly a diffuser as well.)
I don't know about the UK but I run to B&H photography with my questions.  Sorry but they are a .COM.  There staff are TOP tier and very helpful.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It's unlikely I will do that mate - the production values don't mean that much to me - as long as you can see it to the extent you need I am happy with that.

replies.author: @FuhrChris
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering A diffuser can be a thin sheet of Onion typing paper, placed about a foot away from any hot bulbs.
If your not going to add a second or third normal electric lamp (a house lamp), then move the light you used off to one side just enough that you are not getting those weird shadows.
Or
 (Go back to what you were using before.  You must have done something different this time and it really showed.)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @GrandadTinkerer
text:
I spent the last twenty-odd years of my working life using Autocad, mainly to produce schematic and wiring diagrams. 
When I got my 3D printer, I started to use Tinkercad. Initially, I struggled with the concept of using primitives to draw the 3D models that I required. My brain needed a good dose of re-calibration!

replies.author: @FuhrChris
replies.text:
I'm good with Rhino.
But they never got much into the Art side so I had to learn ZBrush (ZBrush Core is still free, I think?)
But They don't do dimensioning so things like gears that mesh are a challenge, or worse.
So I'm looking at building a hard ware software combo of Solidworks combined with Rhino or an Older version of Rhino in which the destructive testing would be Rhino files moved over into Solidworks because I think I can short cut the learning of the base modeling in Rhino. 
With the Zbrush you learn a new way to go about learning.
1. Utilize Facebook groups.  Post your hang ups and someone will get back to you.
2.Utilize You tubes.  Start a Save file with your new quiry like learning "Tinkercad"  In Zbrush I am constantly reviewing You tubes of specific functions that I haven't done in like a year.  No shame even the software designers have been known to say "I"ve not used this combo of button functions in a while!"  There are just that many OPTIONS now days.

replies.author: @rogerwilson6367
replies.text: I,ve used Solidworks for about 20 years and I can't get my head around Tinkercad or Freecad.

author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text:
Thanks, Rob.
The important thing for me when I started was a jargon-buster! I didn't know what 'spline' or 'offset' etc actually meant in the context of the program.
And electronics circuits/boards/simulators may as well have been Klingon opera.
Jargon-busters; save a LOT of trial and error/frustration and they really help people get started.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Absolutely agree mate - It really gets on my nerves when somebody writes a comment and concentrates on exact jargon I didn't use to criticize it especially when the actual meaning was clear. But ah well lol
